citations.narrative:
The licensee has gone over the allowed animal maximum on their USDA license. There were approximately 10,000
animals present at the facility. The licensee is currently licensed for a maximum of 8100 animals.
The USDA license authorizes the maximum number of animals per license. This information helps ensure that all animals
can be adequately housed and cared for.
A licensee must either obtain a new license with an appropriate maximum number of animals or reduce the number of
animals at the facility to below the maximum number of allowed animals.
To be corrected by August 5, 2026.

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The licensee did not use appropriate methods to treat diseases and injuries. The licensee dispatched sick or injured using
a method that is not approved by the attending veterinarian and does not meet the Animal Welfare Regulations' definition
of euthanasia because it does not reliably produce rapid unconsciousness and subsequent death without evidence of pain
and distress.
Dispatching sick or injured animals using inappropriate method may cause unnecessary pain and distress.
Licensee must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries, including an
appropriate method of euthanasia, approved by the Attending Veterinarian.
To be corrected by May 5, 2026

During the review of records, the licensee was unable to provide complete acquisition and disposition records for birds.
Cage cards are used to document births; however, these records are discarded after rearing and are not maintained. The
facility tracks the number of birds sold but does not maintain records of birds that died or were euthanized.
Failure to maintain complete acquisition and disposition records interferes with the ability to accurately identify animals
and trace their movement.
The licensee shall make, keep, and maintain records that fully and correctly disclose information concerning regulated
animals purchased, born, hatched or otherwise acquired, as well as animals transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise
disposed.
To be corrected by May 13, 2026.

Facility personnel stated that they have not reviewed the contingency plan on an annual basis. Annual reviews of the
contingency plan ensure that changes to the facility program are accounted for in emergency planning. The licensee must
maintain and review the contingency plan on at least an annual basis to ensure that the plan is compliant with all of
section 2.134. The licensee must maintain documentation of the annual review including any amendments or changes
made to the plan since the previous year’s review.
To be corrected by June 5, 2026.

Approximately 25% of the breeding cages (primary enclosures) had sharp points associated with water access. Water
access is via nipple waterers that is shared by two cages. To allow access to both cages the wire mesh dividing the cages
is cut next to the water nipple. The cut wire results in a sharp point that could injury the bird.
Enclosures with sharp points can result in injury to the animals.
The facility must remove the sharp points from the enclosures which could injure the birds.
To be corrected by May 21, 2026.

A primary enclosure for fledgling birds housing an approximate 50 birds had insufficient perching space. The enclosure
had two perches, measuring approximately 10 and 18 inches. The licensee stated that some perches had been removed
because the birds chewed on them. A lack of perching puts strain on the bird’s feet and legs. Birds that rest by perching
must be provided with a sufficient number of perches to comfortably hold all the birds in the primary enclosure at the
same time, including birds that are ranked low in a dominance hierarchy.
To be corrected by May 12, 2026.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
Unsanitary conditions were observed in and around budgie cages and on water lines, characterized by heavy
accumulation of droppings, feather dust, seed hulls, and old food. Perches and bars are covered in caked-on waste, and
cage floors contain feces-covered broken and intact eggs along with significant piles of dried and fresh feces. Such
accumulation is detrimental to budgie health. Excreta and food waste must be removed from the primary enclosures and
from under and around primary enclosures as often as necessary to prevent excessive accumulation of feces and food
waste, to prevent soiling of the birds contained in the primary enclosure, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests,
and odors.
To be corrected by May 19, 2026.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
Facility personnel stated that they do not sanitize the cages the birds are housed in. Not sanitizing cages can pose a
significant health risk to the birds. Primary enclosures and food and water receptacles for birds must be sanitized as often
as necessary to prevent accumulation of dirt, debris, food waste, excreta, and other disease hazards.
To be corrected by May 19, 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
Additional Inspectors:
Mary Ann Archer, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER
CODY YAGER n

narrative:
2.1(b)(2)(i) 
   
Requirements and application 
The licensee has gone over the allowed animal maximum on their USDA license. There were approximately 10,000 
animals present at the facility. The licensee is currently licensed for a maximum of 8100 animals. 
The USDA license authorizes the maximum number of animals per license. This information helps ensure that all animals 
can be adequately housed and cared for. 
A licensee must either obtain a new license with an appropriate maximum number of animals or reduce the number of 
animals at the facility to below the maximum number of allowed animals. 
To be corrected by August 5, 2026. 
2.40(b)(2)  Critical
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The licensee did not use appropriate methods to treat diseases and injuries. The licensee dispatched sick or injured using 
a method that is not approved by the attending veterinarian and does not meet the Animal Welfare Regulations' definition 
of euthanasia because it does not reliably produce rapid unconsciousness and subsequent death without evidence of pain 
and distress. 
Dispatching sick or injured animals using inappropriate method may cause unnecessary pain and distress. 
Licensee must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries, including an 
appropriate method of euthanasia, approved by the Attending Veterinarian. 
To be corrected by May 5, 2026 
2.75(b)(1) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
During the review of records, the licensee was unable to provide complete acquisition and disposition records for birds. 
Cage cards are used to document births; however, these records are discarded after rearing and are not maintained. The 
facility tracks the number of birds sold but does not maintain records of birds that died or were euthanized. 
Failure to maintain complete acquisition and disposition records interferes with the ability to accurately identify animals 
and trace their movement. 
The licensee shall make, keep, and maintain records that fully and correctly disclose information concerning regulated 
animals purchased, born, hatched or otherwise acquired, as well as animals transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise

disposed.  
To be corrected by May 13, 2026. 
2.134(b) 
   
Contingency planning. 
Facility personnel stated that they have not reviewed the contingency plan on an annual basis. Annual reviews of the 
contingency plan ensure that changes to the facility program are accounted for in emergency planning. The licensee must 
maintain and review the contingency plan on at least an annual basis to ensure that the plan is compliant with all of 
section 2.134. The licensee must maintain documentation of the annual review including any amendments or changes 
made to the plan since the previous year’s review.  
To be corrected by June 5, 2026. 
3.153(a)(1)(i) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
Approximately 25% of the breeding cages (primary enclosures) had sharp points associated with water access. Water 
access is via nipple waterers that is shared by two cages. To allow access to both cages the wire mesh dividing the cages 
is cut next to the water nipple. The cut wire results in a sharp point that could injury the bird.  
Enclosures with sharp points can result in injury to the animals. 
The facility must remove the sharp points from the enclosures which could injure the birds. 
To be corrected by May 21, 2026. 
3.153(a)(2) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
A primary enclosure for fledgling birds housing an approximate 50 birds had insufficient perching space. The enclosure 
had two perches, measuring approximately 10 and 18 inches. The licensee stated that some perches had been removed 
because the birds chewed on them. A lack of perching puts strain on the bird’s feet and legs. Birds that rest by perching 
must be provided with a sufficient number of perches to comfortably hold all the birds in the primary enclosure at the 
same time, including birds that are ranked low in a dominance hierarchy.  
To be corrected by May 12, 2026. 
3.158(a)(1) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
Unsanitary conditions were observed in and around budgie cages and on water lines, characterized by heavy 
accumulation of droppings, feather dust, seed hulls, and old food. Perches and bars are covered in caked-on waste, and 
cage floors contain feces-covered broken and intact eggs along with significant piles of dried and fresh feces. Such 
accumulation is detrimental to budgie health. Excreta and food waste must be removed from the primary enclosures and 
from under and around primary enclosures as often as necessary to prevent excessive accumulation of feces and food 
waste, to prevent soiling of the birds contained in the primary enclosure, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, 
and odors.  
To be corrected by May 19, 2026.

3.158(b)(1) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
Facility personnel stated that they do not sanitize the cages the birds are housed in. Not sanitizing cages can pose a 
significant health risk to the birds. Primary enclosures and food and water receptacles for birds must be sanitized as often 
as necessary to prevent accumulation of dirt, debris, food waste, excreta, and other disease hazards.  
To be corrected by May 19, 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Mary Ann Archer, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER
